text:
This paper is an extension of our work presented at the IEEE GLOBECOM
Conference in 2024 [1]
This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation
of China (NSFC) under Grant 62271121,  the National Key R&D Program
of China under Grant 2024YFE0200400 and SQ2024YFE0200402, and the
National Key Laboratory of Wireless Communications Foundation under
Grant 2023KP01605. (Corresponding author: Lingxiang Li)
T. Hu, S. Wang, Y. Xie, L. Li, Z. Chen, and B. Ning are with University
of Electronic Science  and Technology of China  (UESTC),  China. W.
Hamidouche, L. Bariah,  S. Lasaulce,  and M. Debbah are with Khalifa
University, UAE. T. Hu  is also with Khalifa University, UAE.  (e-mail:
huty@std.uestc.edu.cn;  shuaiwang@uestc.edu.cn;  xieyh@std.uestc.edu.cn;
lingxiang li uestc@hotmail.com;  chenzhi@uestc.edu.cn;  boyd-
ning@outlook.com;  {wassim.hamidouche,  lina.bariah,  samson.lasaulce,
merouane.debbah@ku.ac.ae}).

1
Map Construction and
Integrated Generative
in ISAC
ai Wang, Member, IEEE, Yunhang Xie,
r Member, IEEE, Boyu Ning, Member, IEEE,
EE, Lina Bariah, Senior Member, IEEE,
Me´rouane Debbah, Fellow, IEEE
Fig. 1: THz radio maps with the same beam direction, where
each block represents an obstacle and different colors indicate
the strength of RSS. (a) Without the lower-left obstacle. (b)
With the lower-left obstacle.
N  recent  years,  terahertz  (THz)  communication  and
integrated  sensing  and  communication  (ISAC)  have
I
emerged as key enablers of  future  sixth-generation  (6G)
wireless networks, offering advanced capabilities in terms of
high-speed data transmission and environmental sensing [2]–
[6]. The intrinsic characteristics of THz signals, marked by
narrow directional beams and weak diffraction performance,
expose them to frequent obstruction by obstacles [7], thereby
negatively impacting the coverage and quality of THz com-
munication signals. Consequently,  the demand for obstacle
sensing with THz ISAC becomes imperative. The blockage
sensitivity of THz signals facilitates obstacle sensing, while the
sensed obstacles, in turn, enable THz communication systems
to proactively mitigate link disruptions through strategic in-
terventions such as beam switching, relaying, or hand-off [8]–
[11], thereby achieving both integration and coordination gains
in ISAC.
Radio maps, which capture channel knowledge of received
signal  strength (RSS) across all  transceiver  locations, pro-
vide valuable insight  into the propagation characteristics of
wireless signals affected by the physical environment, partic-
ularly obstacles [1], [12]–[16]. In this context, the radio map
generated during communication effectively represents  the
radio environment,  facilitating applications such as network
optimization and beam interventions.  Intuitively,  since  the
strength of RSS, affected by obstacles, provides direct access
to information about the shape and location of obstacles, the
radio environment characterized by RSS distribution can be
utilized for obstacle sensing, enabling wireless communication
systems to possess sensing capabilities,  i.e., communication-

assisted sensing in ISAC.
Given the blockage sensitivity and directionality of THz
beams [1], [16], THz radio maps are particularly well-suited
for obstacle sensing. This is because the RSS strength mea-
sured in the THz band is highly correlated with the presence of
obstacles within the beam’s range. Moreover, THz radio maps
that vary with beam direction share the same environmental
semantics  (i.e.,  the same obstacle  layout)  across different
directions. Note that since THz radio maps are determined by
beam directions, we also refer to them as directional maps.
This is illustrated in Fig. 1, which shows such maps with
directionality. From the areas outlined by the black circle in
Fig. 1,  it can be observed that the obstacle in the lower-left
corner of Fig. 1(b) does not appear in Fig. 1(a), demonstrating
the attenuation effect of this obstacle on the THz beam and
the corresponding RSS.
However,  in practice,  it  is challenging  to fully acquire
the RSS distribution,  i.e.,  to construct a THz radio map for
obstacle sensing. This difficulty arises from the impracticality
of positioning THz sensors (e.g., user equipment) to measure
RSS at every conceivable location for every scenario requiring
environmental  information.  In other words, only a  limited
amount of prior knowledge of  the THz radio map can be
obtained from the sparsely and randomly deployed sensors.
Overcoming  this  limitation and deriving a comprehensive
propagation model  that enables obstacle sensing with good
generalization (i.e.,  the ability  to perform well  in unseen
scenarios or environments) remains an open research problem.
Although there have been some related studies,  they have
not  fully addressed this  issue.

For  radio map construction,
existing approaches can typically be categorized into model-
assisted [17]–[22] and data-driven methods [1],  [16],  [23]–
[26], where the former assumes that RSS follows a signal
propagation model, and the latter primarily leverages the pow-
erful data mining capabilities of deep learning (DL). Works
such as [24]–[26] used prior physical environmental maps for
radio map construction but did not account for corresponding
environmental sensing. On the other hand, authors in [16]–
[22] proposed jointly estimating a virtual environmental map
and  radio map. However,  their  generalization capabilities
may be limited,  as  they adopt environment-specific online
training and deployment. Additionally, the difference between
the virtual map and the actual physical environment can be
substantial. For THz-based sensing,  [27]–[31] utilized THz
communication signals for  target  sensing within the ISAC
framework. However, such approaches may lack the capability
to accurately depict the radio maps. To achieve both radio and
environmental information, [1], [16] simultaneously addressed
THz radio map construction and obstacle sensing. However,
[16] did not focus on the generalization ability, while [1] failed
to meet  the precision requirements for THz beam strategic
interventions in obstacle sensing.
It is important to note that THz radio map construction and
obstacle sensing are not sequential tasks, but complementary
ones. A more accurate THz radio map enables more precise
sensing, while sensing results can, in turn, improve radio map
construction due to the additional environmental information
they provide. Moreover,  since multiple directional THz ra-

2
THz Sensor
THz BS
THz 
Sensor
(a)  (b)
Fig.  2: Visual  illustration of an example scenario for  the
considered 2D area:  (a) a satellite  image of a section of
Oklahoma, and (b) the corresponding schematic diagram.
dio maps share the same environmental semantics, obstacles
can be sensed based on an ensemble of multi-maps  [16],
rather  than relying on individual maps for separate sensing
results [1]. Motivated by the above, we aim to address the
necessary yet challenging tasks of THz radio map construction
and obstacle sensing in an integrated manner, utilizing the
shared environmental  semantics. Specifically, we formulate
an integrated problem to mutually improve the accuracy of
construction and sensing, proposing a voting-based sensing
scheme to enable ensemble and generalization. To solve this
problem with enhanced generalization, and considering the
powerful capabilities of generative models, we develop an
integration framework based on conditional generative ad-
versarial network (CGAN)  [32],  [33], balancing generation
quality and inference speed. CGANs enable efficient inference
with a single forward pass under the guidance of the input
condition, making them computationally suitable for real-time
construction and sensing. While alternative generative models
exist,  their comparative analysis is beyond the scope of this
work. Compared to state-of-the-art (SOTA) studies [1], [16]–
[31], which suffer  from limited generalization and fail  to
balance accurate sensing and construction, our contributions
address these challenges and are summarized as follows:
• We formulate an integrated problem for THz radio map
construction and obstacle sensing, which not only consid-
ers the probability distribution of obstacle layouts but also
incorporates the shared environmental semantics across
different directional THz radio maps. To the best of the
authors’ knowledge, this is the first time these two tasks
have been addressed in an integrated manner.
• We propose a CGAN-based integration framework to
solve the formulated problem and achieve THz ISAC,
where the CGAN enhances generalization. By leveraging
the objective function of the problem,  the shared envi-
ronmental semantics, and a U-Net module [34] capable
of uncovering the manifold nature of THz radio maps,
the CGAN effectively learns the THz signal propagation
mechanism with respect  to the distribution of obstacle
layouts.
• We propose a voting-based sensing scheme that exploits
the shared environmental semantics, wherein ”hard” and
”soft” voting strategies are applied to different directional
THz radio maps. The proposed scheme facilitates mutual.

For  the system setup considered, we first model  the en-
vironment  to be sensed, along with the corresponding THz
communication scenario. Building on this, we then elaborate
on the signal propagation model and proceed to characterize
the directional THz radio maps using RSS. Given the limited
prior knowledge available about  the THz  radio map, we
introduce a corresponding construction problem and explore
the challenges associated with its exploitation in the sensing
process.

3
A. System Setup Description
As shown in Fig. 2, we consider a two-dimensional (2D)
R2
rectangular area, M= [0; L]×[0;W] ∈  , with length L and
width W. The area contains numerous obstacles with varying
attributes,  such as different  shapes,  categories,  quantities,
and locations, distributed in an organized yet unpredictable
manner. Examples of such environments include dense urban
areas and functional indoor spaces. Note that Fig. 2(a) shows a
satellite image of a section of Oklahoma [35], and the obstacle
layout shown is intended as a representative scenario within
this region. Using a spatial discretization approach [26], we
partition the area M into NL × NW grids, as illustrated in
Fig. 2(b). The coordinates at the center of the grid (i, j) are
R2
denoted as xi,j ∈  , with each grid location corresponding
to the respective center coordinates.
To model the environmental scenarios within M, we rep-
resent  the environmental  layout, dependent on the various
attributes of the obstacles inside, using a random variable S.
This variable S follows a probability density function (PDF)
p(S) defined on a sample space A, which is large due to
the substantial variety of obstacle configurations within M.
Consequently,  this model accounts for all possible environ-
mental scenarios, with each outcome in A corresponding to
a specific scenario characterized by the determined attributes
of the obstacles [36]. Building upon an occupancy map-based
model [37] to describe the environment, we define a binary
NL×NW
matrix S ∈ {0, 1}  to represent the discretized outcome
of S. In this matrix, [S]i,j = 1 indicates that the grid (i, j) is
at least half-occupied by an obstacle (as shown in Fig. 2(b)),
whereas  [S]i,j = 0 signifies  the absence of obstacles  in
that grid. For integrated THz communication and environment
sensing, we assume that a THz base station (BS) is positioned
at  the center of M with location xtx,  functioning as  the
transmitter equipped with a directional antenna. In addition, K
THz sensors, each with omnidirectional antennas, are sparsely
distributed at the centers of K grids, where K < NL ×NW,
typically being small. These THz sensors, acting as receivers,
are responsible for capturing the RSS measurements resulting
from the signals transmitted by the THz BS. To model this, we
NL×NW
introduce a mask matrix Mrx ∈ {0, 1}  , where an entry
[Mrx]i,j = 1 indicates the presence of a sensor within the grid
(i, j), and [Mrx]i,j = 0 otherwise. Therefore, the mask matrix
satisfies ∥Mrx∥0 = K, where ∥ · ∥0 denotes the ℓ0 norm.
B. Directional Propagation Model and Radio Maps
For a given environmental scenario with layout S, we con-
sider a narrow THz beam emitted from the BS, with direction
θd and beamwidth θb,  for communication. To characterize
the power attenuation of the corresponding THz signal as it
propagates from the BS location xtx to the grid xi,j , we define
the function ΓS (xi,j, θd, θb). This function accounts for the
interaction of the signal with obstacles in S, such as reflection
and scattering, along the propagation path. Assuming that THz
beams cannot penetrate obstacles, the RSS at the location xi,j
resulting from the directional THz beam is given by:
2
ΨS (xi,j, θd, θb) = PTΓS (xi,j, θd, θb) (1 − [S]i,j) + σ ,
(1)

2
where PT is the transmit power at the BS and σ  represents the
variance of the additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN). It is
important to note that the THz channel experiences significant
challenges such as wide molecular absorption, distinct scat-
tering properties [38], [39], and high sensitivity to obstacles,
among other  factors. These characteristics make the power
attenuation function ΓS (xi,j, θd, θb) primarily dependent on
the obstacles, and obtaining an explicit expression for  it  is
difficult. Therefore, we employ ray tracing [40] to characterize
this power attenuation, which is further evaluated in Section V.
The directional THz radio map, which illustrates the dis-
tribution of  the RSS for  the environmental  scenario with
layout S,  is obtained by collecting the RSS from all grid
1
locations . Furthermore, by performing beam scanning, similar
to the synchronization signal blocks (SSB) used in 5G NR,
a number Nd of  transmitted THz beams with a set D of
evenly distributed directions of departure (DoD) should be
considered. This leads to the generation of the corresponding
multidirectional THz radio maps. Specifically, D = {θd =
d∆θ, d = 1, . . .

, Nd}, where ∆θ is the angular separation
between two adjacent beam DoDs. To represent the Nd multi-
directional THz  radio maps under  the obstacle  layout S,
RNL×NW×Nd
we define a  tensor ΨS ∈  , which maps all
grid locations within M and all beam directions in D with
beamwidth θb to a set of corresponding signal strengths. The
entry (i, j, d) of this tensor is given by ΨS(xi,j, θd, θb), with
a particular directional radio map corresponding to the d-th
beam direction represented as [ΨS]d.
As seen in (1),  the directional  radio maps are inherently
correlated with the environmental obstacles  through which
the THz signals propagate, which motivates  the use of a
segmentation-based sensing approach [1]. A simple method,
for example, involves directly segmenting obstacles from ΨS
2
based on the condition ΨS(xi,j, θd, θb) = σ , although this
may result in the misclassification of some shadowed regions,
2
where PTΓS (xi,j, θd, θb) ≪ σ , as obstacles. Notably, multi-
directional radio maps offer a view of the environment through
the distribution of signal strengths from various angles, pro-
viding new opportunities to improve obstacle sensing. This
concept has been explored in [16], where obstacles are de-
tected through the intersection of multi-directional THz radio
maps. Therefore, when utilizing multi-directional maps for the
target scenario, it becomes possible to infer the characteristics
of environmental obstacles and achieve accurate sensing.
C. Constructing Directional THz Radio Map from Sparse RSS
measurements for Obstacle Sensing
Due  to  the  limited deployment  of THz sensors within
M,  only a sparse set  of RSS measurements,  represented
by the sparse tensor Ψ(cid:101) S = ΨS ⊙ stack(Mrx, Nd), can be
obtained. This tensor Ψ(cid:101) S captures the RSS with respect  to
Nd beam directions, but  it does not  fully characterize the
complete directional THz radio maps. In this paper, we focus
on constructing the multi-directional THz radio maps ΨS and
1
Note that  the use of high-gain beams prompts us to employ directional
rather  than omnidirectional  radio maps in THz communications, as this is
more realistic in practice.

4
accurately sensing the obstacles S for any given environmental
scenario within M, using only the RSS measurements Ψ(cid:101) S
obtained in the same scenario.
RNL×NW×Nd
Firstly, we define a  function fRSS  :  →
RNL×NW×Nd
, which takes the RSS measurement Ψ(cid:101) S as input
and produces a tensor Ψ(cid:98) S w.r.t.  the RSS estimations of all
grid locations and all beam directions. Therefore, the task of
directional THz radio map construction is to derive fRSS via
solving the following optimization problem:
(cid:104)  2 (cid:105)
minimize ES ∥ΨS −ΨS∥  ,  (2a)
(cid:98)  F
fRSS
s.t. Ψ(cid:98) S ⊙ stack(Mrx, Nd) = Ψ(cid:101) S, ∀S,  (2b)
Ψ(cid:98) S = fRSS(Ψ(cid:101) S), ∀S.  (2c)
K
Given  the  limited prior  data {ΨS(xik,jk , θd, θb)}k=1,  the
⋆
constraint (2b) is introduced, and the optimal solution fRSS
to problem (2) minimizes the RSS estimation error across all
possible environmental scenarios,  thereby approximating the
directional radio maps ΨS, ∀S ∈ A, to the maximum extent.
Recall that conventional model-assisted approaches for ra-
dio map construction rely on accurate modeling of the signal
propagation process. However, achieving this accuracy is par-
ticularly challenging for constructing THz radio maps. The
unique characteristics of THz signal propagation, as described
in (1), and the corresponding radio maps introduce several
difficulties: 1) Uniqueness of  the THz channel: Compared
with statistical  sub-6G channels,  the THz channel’s power
attenuation function ΓS (xi,j, θd, θb)  is more complex and
heavily influenced by obstacles, 2) Directionality of THz radio
maps: THz radio maps depend not only on the environmental
layout but are also significantly affected by the beam directions
and beamwidth; 3) Complex environmental layouts: The com-
plex and often inaccessible nature of environmental  layouts,
along with their intricate interaction with signal propagation
in ΓS (xi,j, θd, θb), adds further complexity. Existing model-
based approaches  [17]–[22], which are explicitly designed
for the sub-6G band, may lead to model mismatches when
applied to THz radio maps,  significantly undermining their
effectiveness.
In contrast, data-driven approaches hold promise  in cir-
cumventing  the model mismatch  issue. These approaches
yield desirable performance due  to DL’s strong capability
for complex function approximation [1], [23]–[26]. The key
idea  is  to directly  learn  the complex signal  propagation
process ΓS (xi,j, θd, θb) from historical RSS measurements
across a variety of environmental scenarios in A, and then
use  this knowledge  to  infer  the  radio maps  for  a  target
environmental scenario. Building on this idea, a deep neu-
ral network (DNN) with a carefully designed structure can
be trained to address the intractable functional optimization
in (2).

This is achieved through standard DNN training on a
set of historical measurements from different environmental
scenarios. Consequently,  the solution fRSS is represented by
the trained DNN. Specifically, for a given target environmental
scenario,  the directional THz radio maps are estimated and
can subsequently be used for obstacle sensing tasks such as
segmentation [1] and/or intersection detection [16]. While this.

metadata.section_title: Integrated Directional Radio Map Construction and Obstacle Sensing

text:
In this section,  to address the challenge of poor general-
ization, we propose an integrated paradigm for constructing
directional  radio maps and sensing obstacles,  inspired by
data-driven approaches. This approach is motivated by the
observation  that  the construction of multi-directional THz
radio maps and the obstacle sensing process should mutu-
ally support each other. In essence, as discussed earlier,  the
accurate construction of multi-directional THz radio maps,
which share the same environmental semantics, should lead to
more precise obstacle sensing. Additionally,  leveraging prior
information about sensed obstacles is crucial  for mitigating
errors in the construction of multi-directional THz radio maps.
Therefore,  rather  than relying solely on a data-driven map
construction method, which faces challenges related to poor
generalization, we integrate the tasks of constructing multi-
directional THz radio maps and obstacle sensing into a uni-
fied problem formulation. This  integrated approach is  then
addressed using sophisticated generative adversarial network
(GAN)-based deep  learning  techniques.  It  is  important  to
note that  these two novel elements,  the integration of tasks
and the use of GANs, effectively mitigate the issue of poor
generalization (which will be further elaborated in Section IV),
thereby enhancing the performance of both tasks. To further
substantiate the motivation for using multi-directional THz
radio maps  in obstacle sensing, we adopt  the concept of
ensemble learning [43] to validate our intuition. In addition,
we reformulate the problem (2) as a guide for the subsequent
design of  the GAN model.  It  is worth noting  that more
precise obstacle information can assist  the GAN model  in
generating more accurate radio maps, which, in turn, enhances
the performance of  the ensemble learning-inspired obstacle
sensing, thereby achieving the integration goal.
A. Generic Problem and Design Considerations
RNL×NW×Nd  NL×NW
Let  us define fOCC  :  → {0, 1}
as a function that maps the estimated multi-directional THz
radio maps to an estimated occupancy map Sˆ for any given
environmental  scenario. The general problem of  integrated

5
THz radio map construction and obstacle sensing is  then
formulated as:
(cid:104)  2  (cid:105)

The equality constraint (3b) represents the process of obstacle
sensing derived from multi-directional THz radio maps, while
C(S, Sˆ)  is a loss function that quantifies  the dissimilarity
between the two inputs. The generic problem formulation (3)
captures the integrated tasks of THz radio map construction
and obstacle sensing due  to the  inclusion of  the equality
constraint (3b) and the loss C(S, Sˆ). These two elements work
together to impose prior knowledge of environmental obstacles
S on the constructed multi-directional THz radio maps Ψ(cid:98) S,
thereby making the two tasks complementary and mutually
reinforcing.
Intuitively, for any environmental scenario S within A,  if
the function fRSS can generate the correct multi-directional
THz radio maps Ψ(cid:98) S from sparse historical measurements Ψ(cid:101) S,
then ΨS will be perfectly recovered, and the obstacle sensing
task will  succeed as well  [1],  [16].  In practice,  however,
the construction of multi-directional THz radio maps will
inevitably be imperfect due to the challenge of accurately
learning the complex THz signal propagation process under
the obstacle layout distribution p (S). This  insufficient un-
derstanding of the propagation process prevents conventional
data-driven approaches from effectively generalizing to unseen
scenarios.  In contrast,  the  loss C(S, Sˆ),  introduced in the
integrated problem, penalizes errors in obstacle sensing that
stem from the construction errors. This compels the function
fRSS to generate multi-directional THz radio maps Ψ(cid:98) S that
contain accurate environmental  information. As a result,  the
integration of these tasks allows for a more comprehensive and
refined characterization of the THz propagation process from
the perspective of obstacles, thereby improving generalization
and achieving greater accuracy in both Ψ and Sˆ.
(cid:98) S
Although the formulation of problem (3) is well-motivated,
there are still  two unresolved issues. The first  issue con-
cerns  the  instantiation of  function fOCC,  as  the accuracy
requirements for such mutual  tasks are not fully clear. Fur-
thermore, noise-driven errors complicate  the  integration of
these tasks. Specifically, poor construction of Ψ(cid:98) S may hinder
obstacle sensing, while erroneous sensing results can degrade
the accuracy of  the directional THz radio maps, ultimately
impacting generalization.

metadata.section_title: Integrated Directional Radio Map Construction and Obstacle Sensing

text:
Moreover, environmental noise can
obscure the distinction between obstacles and regions where
2
PTΓS (xi,j, θd, θb) ≪ σ , which could lead to sensing errors
in fOCC,  even when ideal maps ΨS are used. Therefore,
fOCC must be designed to account for these interdependencies,
mitigating the negative effects of integration errors and ensur-
ing reliable sensing performance. Without this consideration,
the solution to (3) may not be satisfactory. To address this
challenge, we incorporate a voting-based sensing solution from
ensemble learning [43] into the design of fOCC, which will be
further elaborated later. The second issue pertains to the for-
mulation of the loss function C(S, Sˆ). It must recognize that

obstacle sensing is fundamentally a classification task for each
grid while also considering the mutual accuracy requirements
of both tasks. To this end, we utilize cross-entropy to define
C(S, Sˆ), which will be analyzed in Section III-C.
B. Voting-based Obstacle Sensing
By aggregating the outputs of several well-trained learners
with sufficient diversity, ensemble learning [43] can improve
overall performance and mitigate the potential weaknesses of
individual  learners. Inspired by this principle, we propose a
voting-based sensing scheme, which defines two key functions:
RNL×NW  NL×NW
an  intermediate  functionfi  :  → {0, 1}
NL×NW×Nd
and a voting strategy function fENS : {0, 1}  →
NL×NW
{0, 1}  , within  the overall  sensing  function fOCC.
Specifically,  the proposed scheme assumes  that  the  func-
tion f generates N preliminary sensing results {Sˆ  }  ,

Nd
as fOCC(Ψ(cid:98) S) = fENS({fi([Ψ(cid:98) S]d)}d=1). Moreover,  through
the ensemble approach,  the function fENS may mitigate the
negative effects that arise from or are imposed on the THz
radio maps during the integration process. Notably, the moti-
vation for the design of function fi stems from the blockage
sensitivity and directionality of THz beams [1], while the
rationale behind fENS arises from the shared environmental
semantics across the directional THz radio maps of different
beam directions [16].
Naturally,  the  preliminary  occupancy  estimations
{[Sˆ  ]  }  ,  corresponding  to  the  directional  THz

radio maps {[Ψ(cid:98) S]d}d=1,  can be  interpreted as Nd votes
regarding the occupied or unoccupied state of  the grid cell
(i, j). Consequently, the primary challenge lies in the design
of the voting strategy function fENS to effectively aggregate
these Nd votes.  In ensemble learning,  it  is crucial  that  the
learners are well-trained to ensure effective aggregation of
their outputs. Similarly, before designing the function fENS,
we assume that  the sensing performance of  the function fi
with respect  to each directional THz radio map is superior
to that of a blind guessing estimator. That  is, we assume
the following error probability for the preliminary occupancy

It  follows from (5)  that as the number Nd of beam di-

i,j
eventually saturates to zero. Expanding (5) from the grid cell
(i, j)  to all grids  in the region M, we can conclude that
Nd
leveraging Nd directional THz radio maps {[Ψ(cid:98) S]d}d=1  is
advantageous for reducing the error in obstacle sensing. Even
Nd
if  the quality of {[Ψ(cid:98) S]d}d=1  is suboptimal, as long as the
average error probability of Ψ(cid:98) S is less than 0.5, the obstacle
sensing accuracy can still be maintained through the voting
strategy. This approach also mitigates the risk that poor sensing
quality would degrade subsequent construction performance.
To ensure reliability, we multiply C(S, Sˆ) by an indicator
η, which reflects the preliminary sensing performance, where
η = 1 if the error probability ϵ < 0.5 is achievable, and η = 0
otherwise.

metadata.section_title: Integrated Directional Radio Map Construction and Obstacle Sensing

text:
In this case, voting-based sensing and integration
are only performed when η = 1.
However, majority voting with directional THz radio maps
may not be suitable for obstacle sensing, as it neglects the
relationship between obstacles and RSS. For instance, with a
large obstacle, most beam directions may cause a region to be
shadowed in the radio maps, leading to its misclassification as
part of the obstacle during majority voting, even if the region is
not actually occupied. To improve the credibility of the voting
results, we adopt the voting strategy from [16], where a grid
is considered occupied only if  it  receives unanimous votes
{[Sˆ  ]  }  indicating occupation. Specifically,
θd i,j  θd∈D

To verify the validity of the insight obtained from (5) under
the voting strategy described in (6), we conduct a simple
′
experiment, as shown in Fig. 3, using N obstacle layouts
and the corresponding THz radio maps.  In this experiment,
ˆ  2  ˆ

otherwise, where 0 < ε ≪ 1. Fig. 3 illustrates the MSE,
1 (cid:80)  ˆ 2
defined as N′  ∥S − S∥F, as a function of the number of
preliminary occupancy estimations Nd (i.e.,  the number of
beam directions). The results show that  the MSE decreases
as Nd increases. This trend aligns with the theoretical insight,
despite the presence of one or two outliers due to the inherent
randomness in beam directions.
Nevertheless, the ”hard” voting strategy in (6), along with
the discrete sensing results  [Sˆ]  ∈ {0, 1} and [Sˆ  ]  ∈
i,j  θd i,j
{0, 1},  is  inherently non-differentiable, which may lead to
discontinuous gradients.  In other words,  the applicability of.

To address the complex problem outlined in (9), we lever-
age a deep learning model based on CGANs,  utilizing a
finite set of environmental  scenarios derived from histori-
cal measurements. Specifically, we propose a CGAN-based
framework for solving the optimization problem in (9a). This
approach involves offline adversarial training combined with
online inference, where ”soft” and ”hard” voting strategies are
employed to achieve a well-performing model for integrated
tasks,  such as directional THz radio map construction and
obstacle sensing. It is important to highlight that the proposed
CGANs framework is adept at capturing the complex rela-
tionships between obstacle layouts, sparse RSS measurements,
and directional THz radio maps. This capability arises from
CGAN’s strong generalization power and its integration ap-
proach, which extends beyond conventional techniques. In the
context of generative models, GANs strikes a balance between
accuracy, efficiency, and stability due to its conditional nature,
making it a robust choice for learning the THz signal propa-
gation mechanism in relation to varying obstacle distributions.
Moreover,  the adversarial training process of CGAN reflects

Generator of CGA

Based on U-net

Sparse RSS   Simultaneous Construction and Sensing:

Discriminator of C

by (8

Fig. 4: The proposed CGAN architecture for integrate
the synergistic enhancement between map construction and
obstacle  sensing,  offering a  solution  to  the challenge of
balancing regression and classification tasks. Consequently,
the proposed CGAN-based framework demonstrates excellent
generalization performance across diverse target environmen-
tal scenarios.
A. The Proposed CGAN Architecture
The CGAN structure [33] can be utilized to address the
objective (9a) by performing the two integrated tasks. In line
with CGAN,  the proposed architecture consists of  two key
sub-networks:  the generator G and the discriminator D, as
illustrated in Fig. 4. To mitigate the computational complexity
of our architecture and relax the framework’s dependency
on the number Nd of beams during inference, we interpret
the beam dimension of ΨS as  the batch dimension rather
than as a structural component. For a given environmental
scenario,  the generator G treats the RSS measurements (fol-
lowing the same preprocessing steps and predefined threshold
Ψsmax for obstacle segmentation as in [1]) as the conditional
input. It  then generates Nd directional THz radio maps and
obstacle sensing results during  training or  inference,  i.e.,
{Ψ , Sˆ} = G(Ψ ;Θ ), where Θ  represents  the model
(cid:98) S  (cid:101) S  g  g
parameters. The discriminator D functions as a classifier,
assessing the plausibility of  the outputs generated by G in
relation to the real directional THz radio maps and the obstacle
layout. The two sub-networks are trained in an adversarial
manner to solve the problem outlined in (9). This adversarial
training not only ensures that the outputs of the generator G
are realistic, but also enhances the generalization capability
of G (see Sec. IV-B for details). In addition to the adversarial

8
N
Training
Average & ReLU (8)

Based on Encoder
)
mination
d THz radio map construction and obstacle sensing.
training, the structures of both G and D are carefully designed
to further improve generalization, as discussed below.
1) CGAN Generator: The generator G is composed of two
modules: the simultaneous construction and sensing module,
which implements the function fRSS, and the voting-based
sensing module, which performs  the  function fENS. The
simultaneous construction and sensing module employs an
enhanced encoder-decoder structure, specifically U-Net [34],
to effectively capture the low-dimensional manifold nature of
directional THz radio maps. Furthermore, according to (7),
this module simultaneously enables the segmentation of ob-
Nd
stacles from the constructed radio maps {[Ψ(cid:98) S]d}d=1 in each
direction,  although obstacle segmentation is not performed
during the integration phase. As shown in Fig. 5, the architec-
ture of this module consists of an approximately symmetric
encoder-decoder structure with skip connections.

The encoder
compresses each element of  the batch,  i.e.,  the conditional
input [Ψ(cid:101) S]d, into a low-dimensional vector, while the decoder
transforms this vector into the estimated radio map [Ψ(cid:98) S]d.
It  is important  to note that  the encoder-decoder structure
is well-suited for directional THz  radio map construction
because the radio maps are distributed on a low-dimensional
manifold embedded within a high-dimensional  space [26].
This manifold can be  represented by  the aforementioned
low-dimensional vector, which enhances  the generalization
ability of the radio map construction module when properly
trained on target  environmental  scenarios.  In addition,  the
skip connection in the U-Net enables the network to accom-
modate various receptive field sizes,  facilitating the sensing
of obstacles of different  sizes. Since convolutional  neural
networks (CNNs) exploit the spatial local correlations of radio
maps and obstacle layouts with translational  invariance,  the

U-Net  structure  is  implemented using 2-dimensional  (2D)
CNNs, as shown in Fig. 5. Furthermore, the sigmoid activation
−x −1
function, sigmoid(x) = (1+e  )  , is applied after the final
convolutional layer to ensure that [Ψ(cid:98) S]i,j,d ∈ [0, 1].
The voting-based sensing module is designed to implement
both the ”soft” and ”hard” voting strategies, as defined in (8)
and (6),  to generate more accurate obstacle sensing results
during training and inference, respectively. In particular,  the
”soft” voting strategy is utilized to facilitate gradient-based
optimization during CGAN training, where max(0, x) in (8)
is achieved using the ReLU activation function. In contrast,
during inference, where gradients are not required, the ”hard”
voting strategy  is employed, which does not  compromise
the performance of the system. As depicted in the proposed
architecture shown in Fig. 4,  this module explicitly features
separate branches  for  the different  voting strategies. The
generator’s outputs are accordingly induced, providing both
the estimated radio maps Ψ(cid:98) S and the associated occupancy
confidence levels during training, as well as discrete estima-
tions during inference for all grid points. Furthermore,  the
input of this module corresponds to the output of the simul-
taneous construction and sensing module. Specifically, we set
ˆ  Nd
{Sθd}θd∈D = {[Ψ(cid:98) S]d}d=1 based on the preprocessing method
and subsequent segmentation approach described in [1], as
discussed in Section III-B. In summary, this module performs
environmental  semantics extraction via  the voting strategy
f  applied to the set {Sˆ  }  , which is separately sensed
ENS  θd  θd∈D
by the U-Net module.
2) CGAN Discriminator: The discriminator D with param-
eters Θd aims to discriminate whether its input consists of syn-
thetic data from the generator or actual data from the available
historical measurements during offline training. Furthermore,
the discrimination results are utilized to ensure the gener-
alization capability and assist  in improving the generation
capability. Therefore, considering our integrated tasks for THz
radio map construction and obstacle sensing, the discriminator
of CGAN is designed as an integration discrimination module
in Fig. 4, where the input data includes the conditional term
Ψ(cid:101) S, the regression terms Ψ(cid:98) S versus ΨS, and the classification
terms Sˆ versus S. That  is, with the assistance of Ψ ,  the
(cid:101) S
discrimination of THz radio maps and obstacles is conducted.
Specifically, we  set D(Ψ , stack(Sˆ, N )  | Ψ ;Θ )  and
(cid:98) S  d  (cid:101) S  d
D(ΨS, stack(fENS(S), Nd) | Ψ(cid:101) S;Θd) for the corresponding
discrimination, where fENS(S) = fENS({S}θd∈D)  is cal-
culated by (8)  to contrast with Sˆ, while stack(Sˆ, N ) and
d
stack(fENS(S), Nd) are used to keep the dimension consistent
with multi-directional THz radio maps ΨS. For notational sim-
plicity, the outputs of D are designated as D(Ψ , Sˆ | Ψ ;Θ )
(cid:98) S  (cid:101) S  d
and D(ΨS, fENS(S) | Ψ(cid:101) S;Θd).  In addition, as depicted in
Fig. 5, the discriminator structure exploits the encoder part of
the module fRSS.
B. Off-Line Adversarial Training
To obtain an approximate solution to problem (9),  one
need to train the generator G and discriminator D in the
proposed CGAN architecture with a finite set of historical
t  t
measurements, where Nt  samples with the form {ΨS, S }

To this end,  problem (9)  is approximatedly
reformulated as the following empirical version with Θg as
the optimization variable:

where {Ψ(cid:98) S, S } = G(Ψ(cid:101) S;Θg),  and other constraints are
t
absorbed into (10). Note that Ψ(cid:101) S is obtained by randomly
t  K

W L
the sampling rate for each THz radio map. A straightforward
approach to solving problem (10) is to train the generator G
alone with the same training objective. However, this approach
may lead to deficient generalization performance, due to the
lack of adversarial guidance, which prevents G from capturing
the distributions of ΨS and S.
The  training procedure of G and D in CGAN follows
an iterative process, where the two networks are optimized
alternately and adversarially to each other based on problem
(10), resembling a two-player zero-sum minimax game. The
idea is that the generator G is trained to generate the samples
closer to actual data to fool D, and the discriminator D is
trained to accurately discriminate the generated and actual
data so as not to be fooled. Specifically, at each iteration of
the training process, G and D are updated by alternatively
approximating  the  following sub-problems with multi-step
stochastic gradient descent:
minimize αV (G) − β1V1(G, D),  (11a)

Note that the terms V1(G, D) and V2(G, D) are derived from
CGAN for generalization enhancement, and are given as

A. Experimental Setup
Datasets  and Model Hyperparameters. We  consider
three  types of  2D scenarios, which  include  training and
validation scenarios with a simulated environment, as well
as a validation scenario based on a real-world city. These
scenarios  are  labeled  as  S1,  S2,  and S3,  respectively.

Fig.  6(a)  presents  an  example  scenario of  a  simulated
environment,  where  obstacles  have  random quadrilateral
shapes and positions,  and the number of obstacles  is also
randomly determined. Fig. 6(b) illustrates the obstacle layout
corresponding to Fig. 2(b). For each scenario in S1, S2, and
S3, the corresponding THz radio maps are generated through
ray tracing [40], which models the power attenuation function
ΓS(xi,j, θd, θb) implicitly. Each ray is allowed to undergo at
most one reflection or diffraction, as the THz signal is subject
to molecular  absorption and significant  path  loss. Unless
otherwise stated,  the default parameters for ray tracing and
the scenarios are provided in Table II, which also includes the
hyperparameters of  the proposed CGAN-based framework.
t  t
Based on the information in Table II, the data (e.g., {ΨS, S })
corresponding to S1 is used for offline training, while data for
S2 and S3 are used for online inference, which are employed
to assess performance and generalization.
Baseline  Models  and  Evaluation Metrics.  Existing
approaches do not directly address  the integration of THz
radio map construction and obstacle sensing. Therefore, we
use U-Net-based and GAN-based  radio map construction
approaches  [1],  [23],  [24]  as baselines  to simultaneously
perform both  tasks.  These  are  denoted  as U-NetGAN,
DCRGAN,  and  RadioUNet,  respectively.  The  obstacle
sensing idea from [1]  is applied in these baseline models.
Note  that  the method  from [16]  is not  included  in  the
comparison,  as  it  focuses on a  federated  learning model
and environment-specific  training and deployment,  similar
to [17]–[22]. Once  the proposed CGAN-based framework
and  the baselines are well  trained on scenarios S1,  their
performance is evaluated on scenarios S2 and S3.
For evaluation metrics, we use the MSE with respect  to
1 (cid:80)  ˆ 2
both construction and sensing, specifically N′  ∥S − S∥F
1 (cid:80)  2
and N′  ∥ΨS −Ψ(cid:98) S∥F. Additionally, we also adopt the AP,
originally designed for object detection [45],  as a sensing
metric, since AP used in [1] can be adapted to evaluate the
overlap between the sensed and true obstacles from both recall
and precision perspectives. In our simulations, AP is computed
as the average of the approximate areas under the Precision-
Recall (PR) curves for various intersection over union (IoU)
thresholds  ranging from 0.5 to 0.95 with a step of 0.05,

11

ling rate for different approaches evaluated in S2.
where each PR curve is obtained by plotting precision against
recall at a given IOU threshold. As a result, a higher AP
value indicates that obstacles are detected with minimal omis-
sions and that non-obstacle regions are rarely misclassified
as obstacles, making it a more suitable metric for assessing
sensing performance and addressing the limitation of MSE,
which focuses solely on pixel-wise accuracy and may provide
an incomplete evaluation. Note that the cross-entropy in (10)
cannot be used as metric, as  it only measures distribution
differences. To account for the randomness in neural network
initialization, each experiment is repeated five times, and the
reported results correspond to the average performance over
these runs.
B. Effectiveness of Integration
To evaluate the performance of  the proposed integration
framework against the baseline models, Fig. 7a and 7b present
the MSE with respect  to THz radio map construction and
obstacle sensing as a function of  the sampling rate, while
Fig. 7c illustrates the AP to further evaluate sensing perfor-
mance under the same configurations. As can be seen from
Fig. 7,  the MSE in both tasks generally decreases with an
increase in sampling rate, whereas AP exhibits the opposite
trend, increasing as the sampling rate rises, since a higher AP
value reflects greater overlap and thus better sensing of obsta-
cles’  location and shape. Notably,  the proposed framework
consistently outperforms  the non-integrated baselines under
scenario S2, which differs from the training scenario, not only
in terms of MSE but also in AP. This result underscores the
effectiveness of the integration approach and its corresponding
generalization capability.

Furthermore, the observation that U-
NetGAN [1] and DCRGAN [23], which exhibit significantly
different MSE values, achieve nearly identical AP scores high-
lights that MSE and AP each capture distinct aspects of the
overall sensing performance. In addition, the MSE achieved by
RadioUNet [24] does not decrease with increasing sampling
rates. This may be attributed to the fact  that RadioUNet,
which does not  implement  integration,  leverages additional
prior physical environmental maps that are not available in
our integrated problem.

on performance, based on the mean and standard deviation

of MSE and AP during the last 10 convergence epochs.  In

the proposed  framework achieves  a well-balanced overall

Some integration aspects may perform better in one task but
underperform in another. This balance provides flexibility in
adjusting the integration approach based on task-specific prior-
ities during deployment. For example, when RSS information
(i.e., Ψ(cid:98) S and ΨS) is excluded from the discriminator, both
the construction performance and the obstacle sensing perfor-
mance show smaller standard deviations, suggesting improved
stability. Moreover,  the sensing capability,  as captured by
the mean values of AP and MSE,  improves simultaneously.
However, the construction capability reflected by mean MSE
deteriorates. Across various integration aspects, the trends in
AP and MSE remain notably similar, despite their reflection
of different facets of sensing performance.

strategies on both construction and sensing performance, as

It  is evident  that  the proposed voting strategy achieves

in improving performance. To further understand the impact
of the proposed voting-based sensing scheme with respect to

set D of 18 beam directions.

Regarding the impact of Nd, we consider both random and
regular beam arrangements from D, where the latter involves
selecting Nd evenly spaced beams. Generally, a higher Nd
results in more votes from Ψ(cid:98) S and better MSE and/or AP
performance in both tasks, which aligns with the observations
in Fig. 3 and equation (5). Compared to using Nd = 2
directional THz radio maps for voting, increasing the number
Nd of votes to 18 leads to mean MSE reductions of up to
27.5% and 87.3% in the reconstruction and sensing tasks,
respectively, while achieving a 3.91-fold improvement in AP
for sensing capability. This effect is particularly pronounced in
obstacle sensing, as voting is specifically designed to improve
this task, thereby aiding the proposed CGAN model in achiev-
ing a more comprehensive understanding of environmental
semantics. Moreover, the regular arrangement performs better
because evenly spaced beams provide more comprehensive
coverage in terms of both communication and sensing.

D. Performance in Real-World City Scenario
Under the validation scenario S3 with a real-world city, we
investigate the impact of the number NI of obstacles on the
proposed framework during real-world inference. Specifically,
we sequentially remove obstacles from Fig. 6(b) and analyze
the performance by plotting the MSE for both construction and
sensing tasks in Fig. 9a and 9b, respectively, as well as the AP
for sensing in Fig. 9c, all as functions of NI. Both tasks exhibit
performance degradation, as reflected by an increase in MSE
and/or a decrease in AP, with the rising complexity (i.e., NI)
of the scenario. Notably,  the proposed framework maintains
superior performance over the baselines across different NI.
Fig. 9a and 9b illustrate that the advantages of integration
become more pronounced in complex scenarios, as evidenced
by  the widening performance gap between  the proposed
framework and the baseline models,  further highlighting its
strong generalization capability. This suggests that  the pro-
posed integration effectively mitigates the adverse effects of
increased complexity, particularly in obstacle sensing with
accuracy consideration. However, when considering AP, which
reflects both the precision and recall of obstacle sensing, the
above observations do not fully hold. As shown in Fig. 9c,
when the scenario complexity exceeds a certain threshold (e.g.,
NI > 3),  the AP values of both the proposed framework.

In this paper, we have explored communication-assisted
sensing in THz ISAC, where limited RSS measurements are
utilized for obstacle sensing and THz radio map construction.
To address the challenges of enhancing generalization ability
and balancing accurate sensing with construction, we have
formulated an integrated construction and sensing problem. To
solve this problem with improved generalization and mutual
benefit, we propose a CGAN-based framework coupled with
a voting-based sensing scheme. Simulation results demon-
strate that the proposed framework offers significant integrated
benefits across various generalization scenarios, with MSE
reductions of up to 44.3% and 90.6% for  the construction
and sensing tasks, respectively. The framework also achieves
notable improvements in AP, indicating better precision-recall
balance in sensing. As more THz radio maps from different
beam directions are exploited,  the environmental  semantics
extracted by the proposed voting scheme become increasingly
accurate, achieving up to a 87.3% accuracy improvement in
MSE for sensing, which in turn leads to a 27.5% improvement
for reconstruction due to integration.
